1.synthesis:
The Song of Moses is one of the great poems of the Bible — a 43-verse prophetic ode that simultaneously praises God, indicts Israel, predicts judgment, and promises final vindication and restoration. Moses calls heaven and earth as witnesses before he begins (v. 1), invoking the same cosmic audience summoned in chapter 30. He proclaims the name of the LORD: "The Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice. A God of faithfulness and without iniquity, just and upright is he" (v. 4). Against this flawless character he sets Israel's corruption: "They have dealt corruptly with him; they are no longer his children because they are blemished; they are a crooked and twisted generation" (v. 5).

The song rehearses God's care of Israel in the wilderness with extraordinary imagery: "He found him in a desert land, and in the howling waste of the wilderness; he encircled him, he cared for him, he kept him as the apple of his eye. Like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that flutters over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions, the LORD alone guided him" (vv. 10-12). Israel's response to this care was to grow fat and "forsake God who made him and scorn the Rock of his salvation" (v. 15). The song then cycles through divine judgment, Israel's shame, the nations' mockery, and finally the LORD's vindication: "Rejoice with him, O heavens; bow down to him, all gods, for he avenges the blood of his children and takes vengeance on his adversaries. He repays those who hate him and cleanses his people's land" (v. 43).

Adam Clarke notes that this ode "is allowed by the best judges to contain a specimen of almost every species of excellence in composition" — it is so thoroughly poetic that Hebrew manuscripts write it in verse form.
1.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch observe that the song "embraces the whole of the future history of Israel, and bears all the marks of a prophetic testimony from the mouth of Moses." Its addresses are not the contemporaries of Moses but the Israelites in Canaan after settlement — yet Moses speaks as one who already sees what will come. The title "Jeshurun" for Israel (v. 15) appears only here, in Deuteronomy 33:5, 26, and Isaiah 44:2 — a term of affection meaning "upright one," used ironically as Israel grows fat and kicks (v. 15). The name "the Rock" for God (used six times: vv. 4, 15, 18, 30, 31, 37) is the song's dominant theological metaphor: immovable, reliable, the foundation beneath Israel's feet they carelessly abandoned for rocks that are "not the Rock" (v. 31).
1.literary_structure: The song has a discernible structure: introduction (vv. 1-3), God's character vs. Israel's corruption (vv. 4-6), God's care for Israel in the wilderness (vv. 7-14), Israel's apostasy and ingratitude (vv. 15-18), God's threatened judgment (vv. 19-25), restraint from total destruction for the sake of his name (vv. 26-33), Israel's adversaries exposed as powerless rocks (vv. 34-38), the LORD's declaration of supremacy and coming vindication (vv. 39-43). The pivot is in verses 26-27: God would have destroyed Israel completely but restrained himself "lest their adversaries should misunderstand and say, 'Our hand is triumphant.'" Divine reputation restrains the fullness of deserved punishment.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry notes that the description of God as "the Rock" is the song's dominant image, appealing at every crisis to the unchanging character of God: "God is the rock, for he is in himself immutable and immovable, and he is to all that seek him an impenetrable shelter, and to all that trust in him an everlasting foundation." Calvin emphasizes that the song was designed not merely to condemn but to instruct: "The threatenings and reprehensions" were so sung that "the sluggishness, which as it were stifles men's minds, is sharply stimulated." Adam Clarke observes that the song's accusation that Israel "forgot the God who gave you birth" (v. 18) is the heart of the indictment: covenant unfaithfulness is amnesia about who one's Father is.
1.christology: "The Rock" of the song is identified by Paul as Christ: "They drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ" (1 Cor 10:4). The closing verse of the song (v. 43: "Rejoice with him, O heavens; bow down to him, all gods... he cleanses his people's land") is quoted in Romans 15:10 as a promise that the Gentiles will rejoice with God's people — Paul reads the song's vindication as encompassing Gentile salvation. The divine restraint from total destruction "lest the enemy misunderstand" (vv. 26-27) points to the logic of the cross: God's judgment on sin is fully executed, but not in such a way that leaves his people without a Redeemer — Christ's death is God's righteous judgment on sin that simultaneously preserves a people for the divine name.

44.synthesis:
Moses and Joshua — here called by his original name Hoshea (v. 44) — recite the song before all Israel. When Moses has finished, he gives a final urgent exhortation: "Set your heart to all the words that I am testifying against you today, that you may command them to your children, to be careful to do all the words of this law. For it is no empty word for you, but your very life" (vv. 46-47). That same day, the LORD commands Moses to ascend Mount Nebo, there to view the land of Canaan — "the land I am giving to the people of Israel for a possession" — and to die on the mountain. The reason for his exclusion is recalled: "because you broke faith with me in the midst of the people of Israel at the waters of Meribah-kadesh... because you did not treat me as holy in the midst of the people" (v. 51).

Matthew Henry observes the tenderness of this final command: Moses had asked to enter the land, and God had denied him — "I shall say no more of that matter" (Deut 3:26). Now God gives him what is possible: the view from Pisgah, a last gift of grace, seeing the fulfillment of the promise even though he cannot enter it. "The sight God gave him of Canaan... was a token of God's being reconciled to him, and a plain indication that though his sin shut him out of the earthly Canaan, yet it should not deprive him of that better country." The Meribah sin is recalled not to condemn Moses in death but to remind the reader that even the greatest servant of God is accountable for how he represents the Lord before the people.
44.historical_context: Ellicott notes that the return of the name "Hoshea" (Joshua's original name, Num 13:8) in verse 44 may signal that this section was composed by Joshua himself, or at least that the text's author is acknowledging the change of administration by using Joshua's old name at the moment Moses hands over to him. Calvin observes the significance of Moses and Joshua delivering the song simultaneously to different groups — ensuring that Joshua's authority was as fully established as Moses's was departing.
44.literary_structure: Verses 44-47 record the song's delivery and Moses's closing exhortation. Verses 48-52 shift abruptly from Moses's pastoral work to the divine command to die — "that very day" (v. 48), as if God wanted no unnecessary delay now that the legislative and prophetic work was complete. The recall of the Meribah sin (vv. 51-52) creates a frame with the earlier reference in Deuteronomy 1:37 and 3:26-27, enclosing the entire book of Deuteronomy within Moses's knowledge of his own mortality and exclusion.
44.theological_application: Matthew Henry's comment on "it is no empty word for you, but your very life" (v. 47) summarizes the entire message of Deuteronomy: "Those that make light of it put a great contempt upon it, considering what a real, lively, living word it is." Calvin notes that Moses does not use his last exhortation to commend himself or mourn his own exclusion but exclusively to press the law's importance on the people — the mark of a man genuinely consumed with his calling rather than his reputation. The Meribah sin's appearance here teaches that God's discipline of his servants is real and consequential, even for those who have served faithfully over decades.
44.christology: Matthew Henry draws the great typological parallel: "Moses brought Israel to the borders of Canaan and then died and left them — to signify that the law made nothing perfect (Heb 7:19)... it brings men into a wilderness of conviction, but not into the Canaan of rest and settled peace. It is an honour reserved for Joshua (our Lord Jesus) to do that for us which the law could not do." The view from Pisgah — seeing the promised land but unable to enter — is the condition of the law: it sees and describes the salvation it cannot deliver. Christ, the true Joshua, does what the law cannot: he carries his people all the way in, from death into life, from the wilderness of guilt into the rest of his righteousness (Rom 8:3-4).
